title: St. Menas the Deacon — St. Patrick of Prusa & St. Pudens (Monthly)
narrative:
St. Menas the Deacon and Martyr, St. Patrick of Prusa the Bishop-Martyr, and St. Pudens the Senator and Apostolic Witness

On the nineteenth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy Menas the Deacon, a martyr of the Diocletianic persecution who was a servant of the Church in the diaconal order — the order whose specific calling is not the presiding at the Eucharist but the service of those for whom the Eucharist is the source of life. The deacon is the Church's officer of charity, the one who distributes the consecrated gifts to those who cannot come to the altar and who distributes the ordinary gifts of food and clothing and care to those who cannot sustain themselves. Menas died for refusing to betray the community he served: the sources indicate that he was arrested in part because the authorities knew that a deacon would have knowledge of where the community gathered, who its members were, and where its resources were hidden, and that he refused to provide this information under torture. His martyrdom is the seal of the diaconal vow: the servant of the poor who will not betray the poor, the keeper of the community's secrets who keeps them unto death.

The theology of the diaconate, in the Ethiopian tradition, is grounded in the account of the appointment of the first seven deacons in the sixth chapter of Acts: men full of the Holy Spirit and of wisdom, appointed so that the apostles could devote themselves to prayer and to the ministry of the word. The deacon is the embodiment of the Church's conviction that prayer and service are not alternatives but complements, that the community that neglects either becomes incomplete, and that the person who holds both together — who prays in the Liturgy and serves at the table of the poor, who distributes the Eucharist and distributes the bread of charity — is performing a single undivided act of worship. Menas the deacon who died rather than betray his community is the tradition's most extreme statement of how seriously the diaconal vocation was understood.

Saint Patrick of Prusa is the great discovery of this day: not Patrick of Ireland, who will have his own commemoration in due season, but an earlier Patrick, Bishop of Prusa in Bithynia, whose martyrdom story is one of the more remarkable in the tradition because of the theological conversations it preserves. Patrick was arrested and brought to the hot springs near Prusa, where the magistrate — apparently in a darkly ironic mood — suggested that if the Christian God was truly powerful, Patrick should prove it by bathing in the scalding waters without harm. Patrick prayed, bathed, and emerged unharmed. The magistrate was so shaken that he had Patrick executed immediately, apparently unable to tolerate the implications of what he had just witnessed. The tradition sees in this forced miracle not a magic trick but a Paschal sign: the man who passed through the burning water unharmed is the image of the one who passed through death unharmed, and the waters that did not destroy Patrick are the baptismal waters in miniature, the waters through which the Christian passes and is not consumed.

Saint Pudens receives his monthly commemoration on this day: a Roman senator of the apostolic generation whom the tradition identifies with the Pudens mentioned in Paul's second letter to Timothy — I send you greetings from Eubulus, Pudens, Linus, Claudia — a man of the Roman senatorial class who was among the earliest converts to Christianity in the city of Rome itself. Pudens is celebrated monthly as a reminder that the apostolic mission reached the highest levels of Roman society from the very beginning: the Church that was born in Galilee was dining with senators in Rome within a generation of the Resurrection, and the faith that had been proclaimed among fishermen was finding its way into the homes of those who governed the empire. His monthly feast is the tradition's statement about the universal destination of the Gospel: not reserved for the poor, not reserved for the educated, not reserved for the devout — offered to every human being regardless of social position. Amen.
